main_ingredients: nuoc cham dipping sauce (fish sauce, lime, sugar, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of bun thit nuong cha gio is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of rice vermicelli topped with one to two grilled pork chops, one to two fried spring rolls, fresh herbs, bean sprouts, and nuoc cham dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Pork Chop & Fried Roll With Vermicelli (Bun Thit Nuong Cha Gio)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010527). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB; set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli and mixed Vietnamese noodle dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB record reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 per 100 g for the composite dish entry, which reflects the protein-and-fat-dominant portion of the dish (grilled pork and fried roll components) without the vermicelli carbohydrate contribution being captured in this specific 100 g slice. Because the full dish as consumed includes rice vermicelli — a high-carbohydrate component — a GI of 53 (low) is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI ~53–61, mean ~53). Glycemic load per serving is calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100. Given the HPB per-100g carbohydrate reports 0.0 for this composite entry, GL per serving is recorded as 0.0 with the caveat that the true GL of a full 400 g serving including vermicelli is substantially higher (estimated 20–30 GL units based on ~60–80 g available carbohydrate from vermicelli). This limitation is documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold — driven by fish sauce in the nuoc cham dipping sauce and pork marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request nuoc cham sauce on the side and use no more than one teaspoon, ask for reduced fish sauce in the marinade, and keep the total portion to no more than 150 g to limit sodium intake to approximately 1575 mg from this dish alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a high-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~53–61) and the cha gio wrapper is deep-fried refined starch, both driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the vermicelli portion to no more than 50 g cooked, omit or halve the fried spring roll, increase the proportion of fresh herbs and vegetables, and use dipping sauce sparingly to minimise the combined glycemic and sodium load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying of the cha gio generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and increases total fat; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g elevates cardiovascular risk; cholesterol at 89 mg/100 g contributes to dietary cholesterol load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request grilled pork only without the fried spring roll, use dipping sauce sparingly, and pair with extra fresh vegetables to partially offset the high sodium and fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried cha gio generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; pork contains arachidonic acid; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should omit the fried spring roll component, request grilled pork only, and use dipping sauce minimally; fresh herbs such as mint and coriander provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the pro-inflammatory components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Thit Nuong Cha Gio (Grilled Pork Chop and Fried Spring Roll with Rice Vermicelli) is a popular Vietnamese noodle bowl dish widely available in Singapore's hawker centres and Vietnamese restaurants. The dish consists of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bun) served at room temperature, topped with charcoal-grilled or oven-grilled marinated pork chop (thit nuong), one or two crispy deep-fried Vietnamese spring rolls (cha gio) filled with pork mince, glass noodles, and vegetables, accompanied by fresh herbs including mint, coriander, and shredded lettuce, bean sprouts, and pickled daikon and carrot. The dish is finished with nuoc cham — a Vietnamese dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, water, garlic, and chilli — poured over or served on the side. The flavour profile is a balance of savoury, smoky, tangy, and mildly sweet, with contrasting textures from the soft noodles, tender grilled pork, and crunchy fried rolls. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records this dish at 171 kcal per 100 g, with 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g for the composite entry (reflecting the protein-and-fat-dominant components). Sodium is very high at 1050 mg per 100 g, primarily from fish sauce in the marinade and dipping sauce. Cholesterol is 89 mg per 100 g. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. It is a non-halal, non-vegetarian dish containing pork and fish sauce. It is a staple lunch and dinner option in Vietnamese cuisine and is commonly found in Singapore's food courts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database exact entry for this dish (cr_id G00010527, confidence 0.95). The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice vermicelli as the closest analogous carbohydrate component, with the estimation basis fully documented.
notes: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 per 100 g by the HPB source for this composite dish entry. This is a confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this specific 100 g slice of the composite dish, which is dominated by the grilled pork and fried roll protein-and-fat components. The full dish as consumed includes rice vermicelli which contributes substantial carbohydrate; the HPB entry appears to reflect the non-vermicelli components or a weighted composite where carbohydrate rounds to 0.0 at this granularity. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish as of 2026. Glycemic load per serving is recorded as 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate_g of 0.0 per 100 g; however, the true GL of a full serving including rice vermicelli is estimated at 20–30 GL units (based on approximately 60–80 g available carbohydrate from cooked rice vermicelli in a standard 400 g serving, GI ~53). This limitation should be noted in clinical use. The dish is not halal due to pork content and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
